calendar.matters:
LAW AND MOTION
Off Calendar Jun-14-2024 Denied
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Breanna Jones, Esq., Tel. 415.591.6000, appearing via Zoom for Petitioner Raghav Srivastava (present in the courtroom);
Gretchen Hoff Varner, Esq., Tel. 415.591.6000, appearing in person for Petitioner Raghav Srivastava (present in the courtroom);
Tara M. Steeley, Deputy City Attorney, Tel. 415.554.4655, appearing in person for the City and County of San Francisco.

Hearing re: PETITIONER RAGHAV SRIVASTAVA'S Motion for Writ Of Administrative Mandate.

This matter is contested. Argument is held. At the conclusion or argument, the tentative ruling is adopted as follows:

Petitioner's petition for writ of administrative mandate per CCP 1094.5 is denied.

In July 2023, petitioner applied for cash rather than in-kind benefits under the County Adult Assistance Programs ("CAAP"). (Srivastava Decl., par. 6.) At that time, the Disability Evaluation & Consultation Unit ("DECU") determined whether petitioner was entitled to that relief. (Adamek Decl., par. 5.) But while petitioner's application was being processed, respondent changed its scheme and petitioner became obligated to present his request directly to his assigned shelter. (Id.; CAAP Manual section 97-11.1 ["Clients may request accommodations in their shelter placement based on any unique needs."] (Petitioner's Request for Judicial Notice, 1).) Petitioner failed to exhaust this administrative remedy and administrative mandate is inapposite. (Adamek Decl., par. 6.)

The petition for administrative mandate is denied. CCP 1094.5 does not apply to this case since there was no underlying hearing. "[A]dministrative mandate under Code of Civil Procedure section 1094.5 is used to review the validity of quasi-judicial decisions resulting from a proceeding in which (1) a hearing was required to be given, (2) evidence was required to be taken, and (3) discretion in the determination of facts was vested in the agency." (Martis Camp Community Assn. v. County of Placer (2020) 53 Cal.App.5th 569, 593; DeCuir v. County of Los Angeles (1998) 64 Cal.App.4th 75, 81 ["Judicial review by way of administrative mandate is available only if the decision resulted from a proceeding in which by law a hearing is required to be given, evidence is required to be taken, and discretion in the determination of facts is vested in the agency."]; CCP 1094.5(a) ["Where the writ is issued for the purpose of inquiring into the validity of any final administrative order or decision made as the result of a proceeding in which by law a hearing is required to be given, evidence is required to be taken, and discretion in the determination of facts is vested in the inferior tribunal, corporation, board, or officer, the case shall be heard by the court sitting without a jury."].) Traditional mandate "is the appropriate procedure to test the commission's denial of a hearing." (DeCuir, 64 Cal.App.4th at 82; see also Y.K.A. Industries, Inc. v. Redevelopment Agency of City of San Jose (2009) 174 Cal.App.4th 339, 359 ["the commission's discretionary denial of a hearing under the applicable civil service rules was itself a reviewable determination but through traditional, as opposed to administrative, mandamus."].)

It is unfortunate that the rules changed while petitioner's application was pending, but petitioner fails to demonstrate an abuse of discretion.

The Court to review the proposed order submitted by the prevailing party.

Judge: Rochelle C. East. Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda. Deputy: Jaime. Matter not reported.
(302/RCE)
